description: A cup slips from one hand, and another catches it before it reaches the floor. 'kawepa' intercepts a moving person or thing and brings that motion under immediate control. 'howela' (receive) can happen without interception, while 'phelu' (hold) describes the continuing grasp after the catch.
articulatory_notes: Close the back of the tongue against the soft palate for unvoiced 'k' and release it into open central 'a'. Round the lips and raise the back of the tongue for voiced 'w', then move into stressed middle-height front 'e'. Close the lips for unvoiced 'p' and release them into final open central 'a'.
sound_symbolism: The word opens and closes around a moving middle: the firm release of 'k' gives way to rounded 'w', then the lips meet again for 'p'. That second closure can make the form feel like a moving thing brought to a stop.
usage_notes: A net or other instrument takes 'roe'; what it catches remains the direct object. Used as a noun, 'kawepa' is a catch. Later clauses can say whether the catcher holds, carries, or releases it.

semantic_domains.activity: intercepting a moving person or thing
semantic_domains.physical: bringing a moving body or object under immediate control
